item.title: Re: Operational Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
Just for the purposes of clarity, this is a color version of a movie poster for Sin-jin Smyth.

(And yeah — it is definitely not CC-compliant.)

item.title: Operational Staff Post
item.content:encoded: Image could not be sourced reliably and is assumed not compliant with CC 3.0. As such, it has since been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"No, down boy".

Something about that was especially heart-breaking to me.

This touched on a fear that is deep within my soul… The inability to die while suffering.shudders

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
this story is really intriguing and I personally was emotionally engulfed by the nature of the dogs' loyalty for it's presumed master
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The Loyal Part looks more like an formatting mistake, also a good SCP should bring more questions than it answers, I think it's nice that some aspects of this one remain mysterious.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think this one is pretty bad.

For starters it is never even hinted as to why the Foundation is attempting to neutralize this one (in fact, this one is basically the foundation's wet dream since it is essentially a self contained one) but even if we were to assume there is a secret reason as to why, why would be the foundation's first instinct to outright send an MTF unit in without even testing if the thing is immune to bullets first??? I don't have a problem with files that depict The Foundation committing mistakes but this is outright insane, they didn't even test the thing around and they're sending MTFs in blindly? This could make this SCP (which is not a threat at all since it can be just kept alone) into an outright Keter should you piss it off enough that it, for some reason, would be able to leave the vicinity of 1111-2. (And you can't even argue that they knew it was truly impossible since they didn't even know it was immune to bullets!)

The SCP itself isn't bad, I like it even but the insane way the Foundation behaves in this one takes any resemblance of interest/immersion right off. Definetely a -1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I did not care for this at all. The line break between "The tag reads:" and "Loyal" made it read like the author was pausing for a "dun dun DUNNNN!" moment. This was about the time I stopped trying to take it seriously.

There are a lot of questions that are raised by the story. What is the actual relationship between the man and the dog? What will the dog do if the man actually dies? Ultimately, the writing wasn't strong enough for me to be interested in learning the answers to these questions. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the story here, especially with the hanged man as a would-be active participant rather than just the impetus for the dog. (I did have a similar interpretation as JackPsych and Narsil098 at first, and I feel that would've been a satisfying twist- the tag doesn't say who or what the dog is loyal to- but I can also enjoy the story of the dog keeping its human alive at a cost it doesn't understand.)

One problem with the description:

SCP-1111-1 possesses a white coat and red eyes, both of which glow at luminosities directly proportional to its proximity to SCP-1111-2. At distances greater than 500 m from SCP-1111-2, SCP-1111-1 gradually becomes translucent. Additionally, SCP-1111-1's speed, strength, and agility all seem to be inversely proportional to its distance from SCP-1111-2.

The two bolded phrases mean the same thing- "these quantities go down as the dog moves away from the hanged man." Using two different phrasings for the same relationship in the same paragraph makes the description more confusing without adding anything.

item.title: We Gotta Cut That Rope
item.content:encoded: The facility should shoot the rope down or attempt again to get to 1111-2 and cut him down from that rope. If he’s freed, or at least given a object to stand on if the rope is indestructible, he could neutralize the threat now being able to stand and speak. If that’s not a good enough reason, it would still have to bring up interesting results. Besides, think of 1111-2’s rights!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If you're referencing it biting through titanium armored plating, titanium actually makes sense for use in armor; it's extremely lightweight for its strength, which is useful if you're attaching it to something mobile (that's the whole reason the black boxes in commercial airliners are titanium, because it's the lightest material strong enough to protect the contents).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What does 022-J have to do with this?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Great concept, however I have to downvote for the SCP-022-J. Also, something about the way attempted retrieval was handled seems off.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Moments before D-83011 was killed by SCP-1111-1, SCP-1
111-2's lips can be seen moving, mouthing what appear to be the words "no, down boy". Immediately after the death of D-83011, SCP-1111-2 fell limp and resumed normal twitching."

Bad Dog! XD

but seriously, this SCP is really good. Just the idea of a dog who's protective of its "dead" owner is kinda sad, but who knows why he's constantly protecting it?

+1 for you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like it, but I find a lot of tone issues, no vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Same here. I was convinced that it is some kind of white, one-headed Cerberus, deliberately keeping the hanged man in constant agony. Then came the "down, boy" line.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is one of my favorite SCPs, for reasons… But I love it! I can understand that the dog is a loyal companion of the man, and will not leave his side unless it means to protect him. I think the dog does not want to punish or torture the man, but rather does not realize what its doing. It is only trying to protect its master by all means, and while it will probably obey its master, the man does not have the strength to speak clearly enough to command him. In total, I love it!!!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Am I the only one who thought the dog was there to punish the guy? His proximity to 1111-2 causes the guy a lot of suffering and he tries to make sure no one approaches enough to get the guy down.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While it may spoil some of the mystery, this was written for the SCP-1000 contest, and as such is based on a real urban legend. So if you're curious about origins…

http://www.americanfolklore.net/folklore/2011/07/the_white_dog.html

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is creepy and possibly sad.

Does the man "die" when the dog gets to far away? Is it trying to keep him alive? How did they both get in this state?

Great SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very well done. The dog doesn't seem to realize it's protective nature dooms its master - which makes this an interesting twist from the usual horror cliches.
I upvoted even before the Incident Log, now I simply want to double upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, finally actually got round to upvoting my favourite SCP on the site. Haven't read it in months and I did get a little element of cheesiness slipping through this time but that's probably just in contrast to the extremely scientific approach most people have been taking to skips in the past few months.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Poor little doggie! I love this one and it reminds me of Haunting Ground, one of my fav. game.

However, should Shepard be Shepherd? Or are both spelling accepted?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is one of, heck, the best SCP I have read so far. I just love the idea of the hanging man saying "no, down boy". That really adds in an element of mystery that can't be explained by just stating it. It truly makes you think about what caused this SCP, and the events leading up to its creation

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: O.O

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Saw this picture, immediately thought of this SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good, but it won't hurt anybody if someone shares the link in IRL chat.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think it'll do fine on its own merits.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've seen several people showing discomfort about this skip's rating (while it is over 100, it deserves more and I agree with that). So, Why don't you guys feature this? Just a suggestion.

Well, it is as of this writing. I thought it was a nice coincidence. That is one good doggie. Thanks to you I think I can see where to add the flavor to my writing. I used to think that the scientific tone cannot properly carry the emotional weight of something (unless one is trying to be jarring with it, like the description for 231) so I appreciate what's been done here. I see that an objective description of an object, such as what the white dog can do, can still carry emotion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Understated, haunting, and beautifully executed. Exceptionally well written SCP. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is one of the best stories on this site, and it makes me sad that I only discovered it now. It's seriously just an amazing piece. A dog "protecting" its master while actually keeping him from passing on? It's well characterized, well executed, and deserves more recognition, in my opinion.
This is simultaneously hitting +100 and criminally underappreciated. People should not simply be upvoting this, they should be raving about this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Was re-reading the comments (because I'm a narcissist) and realized that I never responded to this.

I didn't bring it up because essentially, why would the Foundation try? It's a stable scenario as it is, and seeing as how the Foundation doesn't currently have a very good way of containing the dog, doing anything that seriously risks permanently upsetting the situation probably isn't worth the risk.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That must be terrible for a dog to get in the way like that.
No interaction with anyone, ever.
That kind of isolation is what gets to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love this. Like Rioghail said, this just seems to fit perfectly together.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
On my screen, when the collapsible opens it cuts off the caption for the image. If it's not just me, you might want to fix that.

Apart from that minor nitpick, this is a great article. There isn't any particular reason that I can pin down, it just seems like everything fits together perfectly and it creates a very solid, atmospheric SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It kind of gives the "predator" feeling to me. Like it's trying to lure someone in to "save" the man then the dog goes and kills them for food or something.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Like some of the other commenters, I wasn't that interested in the article until I came to the end, with the "no, down boy" line. Then my heart almost broke. I have a soft spot for dogs… Needless to say, upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is arguably one of my favorite articles on the site. Feels solid.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I must say I quite enjoyed this. It was a neat article at first, if rather ordinary, then I came across the line "No, down boy." That did it for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This really doesn't interest - let alone scare - me at all. It's well written, but I simply don't see anything interesting to it. I'm not sure what i'm missing, but i'm downvoting as of now. Barring any major developments in my understanding of the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is just awesome. It gives the kind of atmosphere that would be great for SCP-1000.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: the best 1000 entry I've read so far

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As I said in chat…

SCP-1000-2 constantly jerks and twitches in a manner consistent with those of a man being hanged; occasionally gasps for breath can also be heard. The violence and energy of these jerks is directly proprotional to SCP-1000-1's proximity to SCP-1000-2; as the distance between the two increases, the jerks and twitches decrease in violence and frequency.

This right here - and the implications of it - is worth my upvote.

However, I do wish there was more to this SCP. Hmmm.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not sure why, but I really like this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Posted it in the announcement thread, here it is: http://www.americanfolklore.net/folklore/2011/07/the_white_dog.html

I heard it so long ago I don't even remember where I heard it, then came across it again when doing research for the contest.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What legend or urban myth is this based on?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seriously? Cause that's kind of awesome.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Uh, if it's a mix of a working dog (like a German Shepard is, clue is in the name) and a sight hound (like any kind of retriever) it's called a lurcher.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: For a contest entry with a criterion that already somewhat promotes cliche, and that fulfills Brights challenge (it's a dog what fights good), this is really quite good. A nice atmosphere, generally quite interesting. My main question is: What happens if someone tries to get SCP-1000-2 down? I can see some ways to do so without getting too near SCP-1000-1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You know I actually like this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My 1000 contest entry. Many thanks go to Dr. Madd for fixing the image for me, and the guys who helped me edit it and clean it up. Solid feedback is, as always, greatly appreciated.
